    print('  best',ag.get('best_outcome'),ag.get('best_prob'),'truth_prob',ag.get('truth_prob'),'brier',ag.get('brier'))
PY"

for ev in s.get('resolution_events', [])[-30:]:
    ag=ev.get('agents',{}).get('minimalHarness_gpt-55_001',{})

    print('  best',ag.get('best_outcome'),ag.get('best_prob'),'truth_prob',ag.get('truth_prob'),'brier',ag.get('brier'))

print('rows',len(m),'active',len(active),'resolved',len(m)-len(active))

cols=['qid','resolution_date','title','market_aggregate','num_predictions','my_prediction','my_prediction_date']

PY"

# 2025-12-24 Forecast Notes

Submitted initial forecasts for all 331 active questions.

Main evidence anchors used:
- Carabao Cup semi-final draw: Chelsea vs Arsenal/Crystal Palace winner; Newcastle vs Manchester City. Used Arsenal/Chelsea as most likely Man City final opponent.
- UEFA World Cup playoff draw: Italy path points to Wales/Bosnia; Kosovo path points to Turkey/Romania.
- Grammy nominations: Album of the Year field is GNX, Mayhem, Debi Tirar Mas Fotos, Man's Best Friend, etc.; Kendrick Lamar had the nomination lead.
- TikTok US JV agreement: Oracle, Silver Lake, MGX deal expected to close Jan. 22; CEO unknown, Shou Zi Chew retained as a conservative anchor.
- "Big armada" quote in current corpus explicitly points to South America.

Calibration:
- High-probability forecasts reserved for directly evidenced or structurally constrained items (e.g. Kerala for Nipah, Turkey for Grok ban, Board of Peace, Russia-linked Latvian drone, Taiwan arms sale).
- Most future incident/location/name questions were kept at 0.08-0.35 total mass due narrow answer spaces and weak evidence.
- Sports draw/opponent questions generally use multiple candidates with modest mass unless bracket evidence was known.

Watchlist for next updates:
- Near-Jan questions should be searched first: Chelsea coach, Ukraine reshuffle, Bangladesh T20 venue, AFCON Morocco/DR Congo, Greenland talks, Thailand NIDA poll, Australian Open draw/results.
- If new articles appear, update resolved-near-term questions aggressively; early forecasts are intentionally broad.

## 2025-12-25 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 predictions remained active.

Updates made:
- Morocco injury news: Romain Saiss has a current muscle injury; raised him for the Morocco calf-injury question while keeping Boufal/Mazraoui as backups.
- Bangladesh election reporting: Tarique Rahman/BNP look like government frontrunners; Jamaat-e-Islami described as the main rival after Awami League was blocked, so raised Jamaat for main opposition.
- Thailand reporting: Bhumjaithai named Sihasak as second PM nominee, but election still framed as Bhumjaithai vs People's Party; raised Natthaphong/Anutin and added Bhumjaithai to party-list poll.
- Yemen reporting named National Shield Forces in the Saudi/STC dispute; raised it for the anti-Houthi integration body.
- Syria reporting showed continued ISIL operations; balanced Syria Aleppo target between SDF and Islamic State/ISIL.
- Corrected the March crashed-plane question after rereading background: it concerns South America, not MENA.
- Raised ICE on federal-agency questions after new Maryland ICE shooting coverage.

## 2025-12-26 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 questions remained unresolved with active predictions.

Updates made:
- Somalia/Somaliland: Al Jazeera and Straits Times reported Israel formally recognised Somaliland and Somalia convened urgent discussions/denounced the move. Raised Israel sharply for the question about Somalia's minister saying a foreign country supported separatist regions; also raised Israel for the severed-agreements question, while keeping Ethiopia as a meaningful backup because Somalia-Israel formal agreements may be limited.
- Manchester United: 26 Dec Sky Sports coverage still framed United as Ruben Amorim's side, with no evidence Michael Carrick has taken over. Switched the Manchester derby manager forecast to Amorim.
- NFL: Sky Sports reported both Detroit and Kansas City eliminated from playoff contention. Removed Lions/Chiefs exposure from the Super Bowl forecast, raised Denver/Seattle/49ers, and replaced Jared Goff in the NFC champion QB market with Sam Darnold/Brock Purdy/Matthew Stafford/Caleb Williams alongside Jalen Hurts.

## 2025-12-27 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 questions remained unresolved with active predictions.

Updates made:
- Somalia/Somaliland: Al Jazeera directly quoted Somalia's State Minister for Foreign Affairs Ali Omar calling Israel's Somaliland recognition state aggression/interference. Raised Israel to near-certain for the minister/support-to-separatists question. Did not materially raise the severed-agreements question because the reporting still did not say Somalia had severed any agreements.
- Libya: Al Jazeera reported that Libya's army chief Mohammed Ali Ahmed al-Haddad was killed with other officers in a private jet crash near Ankara, and Turkiye held a military ceremony. Replaced the Saif al-Islam anchor on the Al Jazeera Arabic "killed in Libya" question with al-Haddad, keeping one transliteration variant.
- Manchester United: Sky Sports and Straits Times continued to identify Ruben Amorim as United boss after the Newcastle win, so raised Amorim further for the Jan. 17 derby.
- AFCON/Morocco: Morocco drew Mali; Brahim Diaz converted Morocco's penalty while Achraf Hakimi remained an unused substitute recovering from ankle injury. Added Brahim Diaz and trimmed Hakimi for the final stoppage-time missed-penalty question.

Watched but no update:
- Yemen/STC articles reinforce the Saudi-backed government vs STC standoff, but they did not identify the anti-Houthi integration body more clearly than the current National Shield Forces/PLC mix.
- Ukraine peace-talk articles confirm Zelenskyy/Trump talks in Florida, but not the January three-way talks city/mediator or Ukraine reshuffle names.
- Thailand-Cambodia ceasefire coverage named Thai officials but did not change the NIDA election-poll forecasts.

## 2025-12-28 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 questions remained unresolved with active predictions.

Updates made:
- Marc Guehi: Sky Sports/BBC paper talk said Guehi is expected to see out the final six months at Crystal Palace despite Liverpool/Man City links. Lowered Liverpool and added Man City/Crystal Palace.
- Bangladesh election: Reuters/Straits Times reported the National Citizen Party has allied with Jamaat-e-Islami while BNP is regaining momentum. Raised Jamaat for main opposition; kept NCP as a smaller backup.
- Libya: Al Jazeera's follow-up used the shorter name "General Mohammed al-Haddad" and reported burial in Misrata. Shifted the Libya killing question toward that shorter spelling while retaining full-name variants.
- AFCON/Morocco: Reuters/Straits Times reported Achraf Hakimi is fit for Zambia after missing the opening two Morocco games. Raised Hakimi back above Brahim Diaz for the final penalty question.
- Thailand: AP/Independent reported official PM candidate registration. People's Party's Natthaphong remains the main Anutin challenger; Sihasak is Bhumjaithai backup and Yodchanan Wongsawat is Pheu Thai's main candidate. Updated the NIDA PM poll forecast accordingly.
- AFCON semifinal: Algeria sealed a last-16 place with six points and no goals conceded; raised Algeria as Morocco's likely semifinal opponent.
- NFL: Packers lost again and Chicago clinched the NFC North; updated Super Bowl/NFC QB forecasts to include more Bears/Caleb Williams probability and remove the Eagles from the top-five Super Bowl set.
- Ukraine talks: Trump hosted Zelenskyy at Mar-a-Lago and said talks are in final stages, with a Putin call around the meeting. Added Palm Beach/United States as plausible settings/mediator signals for the January and February Russia-Ukraine talks questions, while keeping Turkey/Istanbul live.
- Bangladesh unrest identity: coverage of Sharif Osman Hadi's killing and Dipu Chandra Das's lynching supplied stronger name anchors for the future mass-protest/death question, though the "police custody" wording remains unmatched.

Watched but no update:
- Somalia-Israel language escalated further, but still no report that Somalia severed agreements.
- Niger ordered general mobilisation, but there was no move to dissolve all political parties.
- Yemen/STC and Thai-Cambodia coverage did not clarify their respective forecast targets beyond existing probabilities.

## 2025-12-29 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 questions remained unresolved.

Updates made:
- Gaza governance: Straits Times/Rubio coverage again described Trump's transitional Gaza plan as a Board of Peace plus a body of Palestinian technocrats. Raised Board of Peace for the Qatar invitation question and shifted the Gaza-body name forecast toward Palestinian/technocratic committee variants.
- Bangladesh election: Straits Times reported NCP revolt/resignations after its Jamaat alliance and said polls had Jamaat close behind BNP while NCP lagged far behind; raised Jamaat for main opposition and reduced NCP/Awami exposure.
- Ukraine talks: Dec. 29 coverage kept the diplomacy centered on Trump/Zelenskyy at Mar-a-Lago/Palm Beach, with follow-up Putin contact. Raised Palm Beach/United States for the January talks venue/mediator questions, while keeping Istanbul/Turkey as the main neutral-site backup.
- Manchester United: fresh Sky/Straits coverage still identifies Ruben Amorim as United boss discussing formation changes after the Newcastle win. Raised Amorim for the Jan. 17 derby.
- AFCON/Morocco: Morocco beat Zambia 3-0; Brahim Diaz scored for a third straight match and Achraf Hakimi returned as a substitute. Kept Hakimi as the top missed-penalty candidate but raised Brahim and added Ayoub El Kaabi.
- AFCON semifinal: Algeria remains the strongest bracket-side candidate after advancing; raised Algeria slightly, keeping Egypt/Nigeria/South Africa live.
- NFL: current playoff coverage has Denver and Seattle in No. 1 seed positions, San Francisco still able to take the NFC top seed after beating Chicago, and Philadelphia's defense beating Buffalo. Added the Eagles back to the Super Bowl top five and raised Brock Purdy/Jalen Hurts in the NFC QB market.

Watched but no update:
- NCP infighting does not yet match the later Bangladesh candidate-eligibility/foreign-citizenship wording, so left that market unchanged.
- Somalia/Somaliland coverage remained consistent with Israel as the separatist-support answer, but still did not show severed agreements.
- Venezuela strike reporting escalated the US campaign but did not clarify the interim-president, oil-proceeds, survivor-transfer, or Starlink questions.

## 2025-12-30 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 questions remained unresolved.

Updates made:
- Yemen: Saudi/Al Jazeera/AP-style coverage reported Rashad al-Alimi cancelling the UAE defence pact, ordering Emirati forces out, and the STC pushing out forces affiliated with the Saudi-backed National Shield Forces. Raised National Shield Forces for the anti-Houthi integration-body question.
- Greenland: Straits Times/AFP reported the State Department approving Danish patrol-aircraft purchases amid the Greenland dispute and noted Jeff Landry's special-envoy role. Raised Marco Rubio and State Department for the requested-meeting and venue questions, while adding Landry as a smaller named backup.
- NFL: Sky Sports highlighted Drake Maye's MVP-level season and New England's first AFC East title since Brady. Added New England to the Super Bowl top five. Rams lost to Atlanta with three Stafford interceptions, so trimmed Stafford in the NFC QB market and nudged Darnold/Purdy/Hurts up.
- Bangladesh: Khaleda Zia died; multiple articles still described BNP as election frontrunner and Tarique Rahman as acting chair. Raised Jamaat slightly for main opposition because a BNP win remains the base case, but kept BNP as a small backup.
- Venezuela: US land/dock strike and blockade reporting increased regime-change pressure on Maduro. Raised Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia modestly for interim president and added Delcy Rodriguez/Juan Guaido as low-probability backups.
- Gaza governance: Al Jazeera/Irish Times coverage described the next ceasefire phase as a technocratic Palestinian administration plus international force. Shifted the Gaza-body forecast toward technocratic administration/committee wording and removed Board of Peace from that body-specific top five.
- Bangladesh T20 venue: new cricket coverage again framed the 2026 T20 World Cup as India/Sri Lanka hosted. Raised Sri Lanka modestly as Bangladesh's requested alternative venue, with UAE/Nepal as small backups.

Watched but no update:
- Chelsea drew Bournemouth and remain under pressure, but no reliable replacement-coach reporting surfaced.
- Man United drew Wolves, but Amorim is still publicly treated as manager and Jan. 17 is close enough to keep the high Amorim forecast.
- Ukraine diplomacy became tenser after Russia's Valdai allegation, but no article named a January three-way talks venue or mediator beyond the existing US/Palm Beach and Turkey/Istanbul hypotheses.
- Near-term measles, Austrian skier, Charlotte attack, Starlink, Nipah, and Sabalenka/Brisbane searches produced no direct new signal.

## 2025-12-31 Update

No resolution events in `../state.json`; all 331 questions remained unresolved.

Updates made:
- Chelsea: Sky Sports explicitly reported Enzo Maresca is in danger of losing the Chelsea job unless January results improve. Increased the probability mass on plausible permanent successors, led by Xavi Hernandez, Jose Mourinho, Kieran McKenna, Roberto De Zerbi, and Andoni Iraola.
- AFCON bracket: Senegal topped Group D, DR Congo finished second and now face Algeria in the last 16; Algeria won all three group games and look strong. Added Algeria as the clear top answer for the team that knocks out DR Congo, with Nigeria as the main later-round backup.
- AFCON Morocco path: the completed last-16 lineup implies Morocco's semifinal opponent is most likely to come from the Algeria/Nigeria/DR Congo/Mozambique path. Raised Nigeria and removed Egypt/South Africa from the top answers for Morocco's semifinal opponent.
- Brisbane tennis: Straits Times said Sabalenka starts Brisbane in a field featuring Madison Keys and Amanda Anisimova. Replaced the older Rybakina/Pegula-heavy forecast with Anisimova and Keys as the leading final-opponent candidates.
- Somalia/Somaliland: Al Jazeera quoted Somalia's president on Israel recognition and alleged Israeli conditions; Straits Times/Reuters separately said UAE facilitated the recognition and has deep Somaliland security/economic ties. Kept Israel first for the severed-agreements question but raised United Arab Emirates substantially and trimmed Ethiopia.

Watched but no update:
- Patriots legal-charge coverage creates distraction but also confirms New England are 13-3 and AFC second seed, so the Super Bowl forecast was left unchanged.
- Venezuela sanctions/arrests increased pressure but did not identify a sworn interim president; kept the Dec. 30 distribution.
- Minnesota/Somali fraud and DHS denaturalisation coverage did not clearly match the "largest federal immigration enforcement operation" state question or the Minneapolis fatal-shooting agency question.
- Measles exposures were in airports rather than a state outbreak at 185 cases; no change to Texas.

## 2026-01-01 Update

Resolution events:
- Chelsea permanent head coach resolved to Liam Rosenior. We had not included him; lesson is to react faster to ownership-network hints such as Strasbourg/BlueCo when a vacancy becomes imminent.
- Ukraine defence minister resolved to Mykhailo Fedorov. We missed him; lesson is to include current high-profile reform/digital ministers as plausible reshuffle answers, not just defence/economic technocrats.
- Charlotte NYE attack town resolved to Mint Hill; Austrian skier resolved to Katharina Liensberger; measles state resolved to South Carolina. These were weak-evidence misses.

Updates made:
- AFCON DR Congo: Jan. 1 bracket coverage confirmed DR Congo face Algeria in the last 16, with Algeria perfect in the group stage and Nigeria likely waiting if DR Congo advance. Raised Algeria and Nigeria for DR Congo elimination.
- AFCON Morocco semifinal: Independent/Straits bracket coverage confirmed Morocco's Rabat semifinal path comes from the Algeria/Nigeria/DR Congo/Mozambique quadrant. Raised Nigeria materially while keeping Algeria first.
- Starlink: Irish Times Ukraine outlook noted Ukraine's army relies on Starlink and warned US policy could interfere with service. Raised Ukraine for the free-Starlink-service country while keeping Venezuela live given ongoing disruption.

Watched but no update:
- Bangladesh T20 venue, Venezuela interim president, Ukraine economic adviser, Morocco calf injury, Greenland talks, and near-term immigration-enforcement searches had no direct Jan. 1 signal.
- Anthony Joshua was discharged after the Nigeria crash with minor injuries; no direct Fury-opponent announcement or change yet.
- Chelsea-related active markets are resolved; the Jan. 1 Chelsea articles were useful for calibration but no longer need forecasting.

## 2026-01-02 Update

Resolution events:
- Bangladesh T20 venue resolved to Sri Lanka. We had Sri Lanka at 0.62, a useful example where host-structure evidence was enough even before a direct request article.
- Venezuela interim president resolved to Delcy Rodriguez. We included her only at 0.05 and overweighted Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia; lesson is to keep constitutional/inside-regime continuity candidates higher when the question asks who is sworn in, not who outsiders recognise.

Updates made:
- Yemen: Al Jazeera reported that Yemen's Saudi-backed government appointed Salem al-Khanbashi to take overall command of the National Shield forces in Hadramout. Raised National Shield Forces for the anti-Houthi integration body and kept small PLC/Joint Operations/NRF backups.
- Morocco AFCON penalty: Straits Times/AFP reported Achraf Hakimi has barely featured while recovering from injury and Ayoub El Kaabi has become Morocco's tournament hero. Trimmed Hakimi/Brahim slightly and raised El Kaabi, while retaining Hakimi as the single top penalty-taker candidate.
- Iran protests: Jan. 2 reporting concentrated protest deaths and a state-TV-cited Basij death around Lorestan and Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari. Moved the province forecast from the older Sistan/Kurdistan baseline toward Lorestan, with Sistan and Baluchestan still a major backup.
- Starlink: no direct free-service article appeared, but Jan. 2 added two relevant contexts: Uganda election internet-shutdown fears with Starlink import restrictions, and Iran protests where outside internet access could become salient. Added Iran and raised Uganda while keeping Ukraine first and Venezuela live.
- Ukraine adviser: Zelenskyy named Kyrylo Budanov as head of the presidential office. This is not an economic-development-adviser role, but it is a near-date Zelenskyy appointment; added Budanov defensively while keeping Kubrakov/Jaresko/Svyrydenko as economic-policy candidates.
- Morocco calf injury: no calf-specific confirmation surfaced, but Hakimi remains the most prominent injured Morocco player in fresh AFCON coverage. Added him as a modest backup behind Romain Saiss.

Watched but no update:
- Greenland/Rubio/State Department, NATO Arctic mission naming, Gaza technocratic body/Board of Peace, Somalia severed agreements, Man United manager, Thailand NIDA polling, and near-term immigration-enforcement questions had no direct Jan. 2 evidence strong enough to move.
- The Budanov appointment is more relevant to political/security coordination than economics, so do not over-read it if new adviser-specific reporting appears.

## 2026-01-03 Update

Resolution events:
- Morocco calf injury resolved to Azzedine Ounahi. We missed him entirely after anchoring too much on visible injury coverage around Saiss/Hakimi. Lesson: for team-injury markets, include core midfielders/regular starters even when the article evidence is sparse.
- Free Starlink service resolved to Venezuela. We had Venezuela at 0.14 on Jan. 2 after reducing it from 0.18; the indirect Iran/Uganda additions were overfitt
[...truncated]

import json,re,os
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('articles/2026/02/08/articles.jsonl')
patterns = {
 'school_shooting': r'(?i)(secondary school|school shooting|fatal mass shooting|deadly school shooting|La Loche|Brampton|Saskatchewan|Canada)',
 'sotu_tensions': r'(?i)(State of the Union|address|Trump.*address|late-February|tensions.*(Iran|Venezuela|China|Russia)|Iran.*tensions|Venezuela.*tensions)',
 'egypt_planning': r'(?i)(Egypt.*(planning|ministry|cabinet|reshuffle)|Rania Al-Mashat|Hala El-Said|planning minister)',
 'gezani': r'(?i)(Gezani|Madagascar.*cyclone|Cyclone Freddy|Cyclone Gafilo|landfall.*Madagascar)',
 'tractor': r'(?i)(Tractor FC|AFC Asian Champions League Elite|Al Nassr|Al Hilal|playoff)',
 'carrier': r'(?i)(USS Abraham Lincoln|carrier strike group|USS Gerald R\\. Ford|USS Harry S\\. Truman|aircraft carrier.*Middle East)',
 'deranque': r'(?i)(Deranque|Quentin|Lyon|Rima Hassan|Macron.*Meloni|French far-right activist|Charles Kushner|Pete Hoekstra)',
 'bangladesh': r'(?i)(Bangladesh.*(election|parliament|opposition|cabinet|minister)|Jamaat|BNP|National Citizen|Sharmeen|Rumana)',
 'rail': r'(?i)(burned cables|high-speed rail|Bologna|Venice|Milan|rail sabotage|Winter Olympics.*rail)',
 'navalny': r'(?i)(Navalny|Novichok|toxin|tissue samples)',
 'pakistan_bomb': r'(?i)(motorcycle bomb|police station|Bannu|North Waziristan|northwest Pakistan)',
 'sudan_market': r'(?i)(Sudan.*(market|drone strike)|El Obeid|Bara|crowded market)',
 't20': r'(?i)(T20 World Cup|Super Eight|Super Eights|Pakistan.*(Namibia|United States|India|Netherlands)|Namibia|India.*USA|boycott)',
 'storm': r'(?i)(Storm Marta|Storm Leonardo|Storm Leo|Storm Kristin|Storm Chandra|flooding in France|Western Europe.*storm)',
 'ronda': r'(?i)(Ronda Rousey|Holly Holm|Kayla Harrison)',
 'israel_bobsleigh': r'(?i)(Israel.*bobsleigh|bobsleigh.*Israel|commentary segment|BBC|CBC)',
 'benfica': r'(?i)(Benfica.*Real Madrid|Vinicius|racist remark|Otamendi|Florentino)',
 'russia_explosion': r'(?i)(military police facility|explosion.*St Petersburg|Gatchina|Vsevolozhsk)',
 'iran_frigate': r'(?i)(Iranian frigate|Sri Lanka|Dena|Sahand)',
 'dutch_ai': r'(?i)(Dutch court|sexual imagery|Grok|Midjourney|Stable Diffusion|DALL-E)',
 'dem_response': r'(?i)(Democratic response|State of the Union|Gretchen Whitmer|Andy Beshear|Hakeem Jeffries)',
 'sa_t20': r'(?i)(South Africa.*T20.*New Zealand|Aiden Markram|Heinrich Klaasen)',
 'lebanon_command': r'(?i)(command centre|Israel.*Lebanon|Hezbollah|Amal|Hamas)',
 'greek_migrant': r'(?i)(migrant boat.*capsized|Greek island|Chios|Lesbos|Samos|Kos|Crete)',
 'afghan_raids': r'(?i)(Pakistan.*air strikes.*Afghan|Paktika|Khost|condemns.*Pakistan)',
 'olympics_medals_hockey': r'(?i)(men.*ice hockey|gold medal|Winter Olympics medal|30 total medals|Italy|Canada|United States|Sweden)',
 'brazil_flood': r'(?i)(southeastern Brazil|torrential-rain flooding|Rio de Janeiro|Sao Paulo)',
 'thai_ammo': r'(?i)(Thai ammunition|ammunition site|explosions.*Thailand|Royal Thai Army)',
 'uk_mosque': r'(?i)(mosque.*axe|tarawih|Birmingham|Manchester)',
}
compiled={k:re.compile(v) for k,v in patterns.items()}
results={k:[] for k in patterns}
for line in p.open():
    a=json.loads(line)
    text=(a.get('title','')+'\\n'+a.get('content',''))
    for k,pat in compiled.items():
        if pat.search(text):
            # score by count of terms loosely
            results[k].append(a)
for k,arts in results.items():
    print('\\n###',k, len(arts))
    for a in arts[:8]:
        content=' '.join((a.get('content') or '').split())
        # around first keyword-ish match
        m=compiled[k].search(a.get('title','')+'\\n'+content)
        idx=max(0, (m.start() if m else 0)-220)
        snip=(a.get('title','')+' | '+content)[idx:idx+520]
        print('-', a.get('date_publish'), a.get('source'), a.get('title'))
        print(' ', snip[:600])
PY"

blocks.content:
- Greenland/tariffs: continued Greenland/Denmark coverage supports Denmark as the target country, even though the immediate crisis may be moving toward a framework deal.
- New Jan. 24 questions: for the Dnipropetrovsk miners-bus attack, kept Pavlohrad first but raised Marganets as a serious mining-town alternative; for the southern Lebanon abduction statement, raised Hezbollah and added Amal Movement as a small hedge.

Watched but no update:
- Indigenous-rights explosive-device city, Greek factory-explosion city, Bisan Owda platform ban, foreign leader Australia-visit threats, Pat Cummins replacement, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Champions League draw and Crans-Montana skier crash had no Jan. 24 naming signal strong enough for a confident move.

## 2026-01-25 Update

Resolution events:
- Indigenous-rights explosive-device city resolved to Perth. I had Perth only as a small tail. Lesson: for Australia Day protest-security incidents, include Perth materially; large rallies are not only Melbourne/Sydney/Canberra.
- Sudan siege-broken state resolved to South Kordofan. I had South Kordofan live but behind North Darfur/North Kordofan. Lesson: when reporting mentions Nuba Mountains/South Kordofan under siege, do not let al-Obeid/North Kordofan coverage dominate the distribution.
- Tariff country resolved to South Korea. I missed entirely by overfitting the Greenland/Denmark tariff story. Lesson: tariff-percentage questions can be trade-policy/Asia stories even when contemporaneous geopolitics dominates headlines; include South Korea/Japan/China when the clue is a 15-to-25 percent import-tariff increase.
- Gaza child evacuation destination resolved to Jordan. I had Jordan as a tail behind Italy/Egypt. Lesson: WHO medical evacuations from Gaza often go to Jordan; keep Jordan near the lead when exact transfer counts are asked.
- Greek factory-explosion city resolved to Trikala. I missed by only using major/industrial-city priors. Lesson: Greek accident city markets can resolve to mid-sized regional cities; include Trikala/Ioannina/Lamia-style locations when there is no direct signal.

Updates made:
- Super Bowl: New England beat Denver 10-7 in the AFC title game, so the market is now Patriots vs Seahawks only. Moved New England narrowly ahead of Seattle, while keeping it close because the AFC game was ugly and weather-skewed.
- Australian Open men: Alcaraz beat Tommy Paul in straight sets and remains setless; Djokovic received a walkover into the quarters, improving his rest/fitness profile. Raised Alcaraz and Djokovic, lowered Sinner after the prior cramp/heat issue pending his next match.
- South Sudan displacement: Jan. 25 article headline says fresh conflict displaced 180,000, but full content was unavailable. Raised Upper Nile substantially because recent South Sudan displacement/conflict coverage most often points there, with Jonglei/Unity/Warrap/Western Equatoria tails.
- Gaza mission troops: Pakistan formally agreed to the Board of Peace/ISF and was reported willing to contribute 3,500 troops, so raised Pakistan behind Indonesia while keeping Indonesia first because the question's phrase is up to 8,000 troops.

Watched but no update:
- IAEA special meeting requester, Fury comeback opponent, Bisan Owda platform ban, foreign leader Australia-visit threats, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus attack, Lebanese abduction group, Champions League draw and Crans-Montana skier crash had no Jan. 25 signal strong enough for a confident move.

## 2026-01-26 Update

Resolution events:
- IAEA special Ukraine meeting requester resolved to Netherlands. I missed by assigning most mass to Ukraine/Russia. Lesson: for IAEA or UN watchdog procedural requests, include small/mid-sized European states and Dutch diplomats when they are quoted; the requester need not be a direct conflict party.

Updates made:
- Tyson Fury comeback opponent: Jan. 26 Sky Sports item named Agron Smakici as putting himself forward for Fury's April comeback fight and tied him to the sparring-cut drama. Moved Smakici ahead of Makhmudov, while keeping Makhmudov live because Fury's manager previously called him a top-three option.
- South Sudan displacement state: Jan. 26 reporting said the military ordered evacuations from parts of Jonglei and that some of the fiercest fighting is there. Moved Jonglei ahead of Upper Nile for the UN displacement figure.
- Carrick/Manchester United first defeat: United beat Arsenal after already beating Manchester City under Carrick. Removed those stale leaders and moved to Tottenham, Liverpool, West Ham, Fulham and Chelsea.
- Super Bowl: opening odds made Seattle a clear favorite over New England, with Seahawks roughly -200 to -235 and a 3.5- to 4.5-point spread. Moved Seattle to 66%.
- Australian Open men: Sinner recovered from the cramp scare and beat Darderi in straight sets with strong serving, narrowing the gap to Alcaraz. Kept Alcaraz barely first, Sinner close second, Djokovic third.
- Grammy Album of the Year: AP preview split between Lady Gaga's "Mayhem" narrative and Kendrick Lamar's "GNX" cultural footprint; resolution criteria ask for artist names, so corrected the forecast from album-title strings to Lady Gaga/Kendrick Lamar/Bad Bunny etc.

Watched but no update:
- Bisan Owda platform ban, foreign leader Australia-visit threats, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus attack, Balochistan secured town, Champions League playoff draw, Crans-Montana skier crash, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Jan. 26 naming signal strong enough for a confident move.

## 2026-01-27 Update

Resolution events:
- Fury comeback opponent resolved to Arslanbek Makhmudov. The Jan. 26 Smakici story was a useful late signal but less authoritative than the earlier manager quote calling Makhmudov a top-three option. Lesson: for official opponent announcements, keep manager/promoter shortlist comments ahead of fighter self-promotion.
- Australia online-threats visit resolved to Isaac Herzog. I missed by focusing on Netanyahu/Modi. Lesson: for Australian planned-visit threat cases, include Israeli president/head-of-state visits, not only prime ministers and Indian leaders.
- Bisan Owda platform ban resolved to TikTok. I missed by overweighting Meta. Lesson: current U.S./Trump-era platform-control and censorship stories can point to TikTok even for Palestinian-journalist account bans.

Updates made:
- Australian Open men: Alcaraz beat De Minaur 7-5, 6-2, 6-1 and reached his first Melbourne semifinal; Zverev also beat Learner Tien. Removed De Minaur, raised Alcaraz, kept Sinner second ahead of his Shelton quarter, and raised Djokovic/Zverev modestly.
- Super Bowl: Jan. 27 reports said Drake Maye may have picked up a right-shoulder issue in the AFC title game, while Seattle remained the clear betting favorite. Raised Seattle to 70%.

Watched but no update:
- South Sudan displacement state, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Champions League playoff-draw questions, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Crans-Montana skier crash, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus attack, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, Grammy Album of the Year, Libya killing, strategic-minerals stockpile and English League Cup final had no Jan. 27 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-28 Update

Resolution events:
- South Sudan displacement state resolved to Jonglei, matching the Jan. 26 evacuation/fighting signal. Lesson: when a UN-linked South Sudan question asks for a very large displacement figure, recent state-level evacuation orders and "fiercest fighting" language can be enough to override older displacement priors.
- Netanyahu visitor resolved to Narendra Modi. I missed by staying with Trump/Rubio. Lesson: when Israel visit questions mention "soon" without a U.S.-specific clue, include major non-Western partners such as India/Modi.
- West African party dissolution resolved to Burkina Faso. I had it as a low tail behind Guinea-Bissau/Mali. Lesson: junta-governance questions about dissolving all parties should keep Burkina Faso competitive with Mali/Guinea-Bissau.

Updates made:
- Champions League draw questions: final league table put Real Madrid 9th, Inter 10th, PSG 11th, Newcastle 12th, Juventus 13th, Atletico 14th, Atalanta 15th, Leverkusen 16th, with Benfica 24th and Bodo/Glimt 23rd. Later active market text explicitly references Benfica vs Real Madrid on Feb. 17, so moved Real's playoff opponent and Benfica's first-leg opponent strongly to each other. Newcastle first leg is now Monaco or Baku from the PSG/Newcastle vs Monaco/Qarabag draw pair. Juventus's tie is against Galatasaray or Club Brugge; because the title says "eliminate Juventus", kept the upset clubs ahead of Juventus despite Juventus being seeded.
- Australian Open men: Sinner beat Shelton in straight sets; Djokovic was very lucky after Musetti retired while two sets up, and Djokovic has a toe blister. Semis are Alcaraz-Zverev and Sinner-Djokovic, with Alcaraz/Sinner close and Djokovic/Zverev as smaller live tails.
- Super Bowl: Drake Maye appeared on the projected injury report as limited with a right-shoulder issue. Raised Seattle slightly again.

Watched but no update:
- Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Crans-Montana skier crash, Pat Cummins replacement, Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, Grammy Album of the Year, Norway corruption, strategic-minerals stockpile, Libya killing and English League Cup final had no Jan. 28 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-29 Update

Resolution events:
- Epstein/Commerce Secretary resolved to Howard Lutnick, matching the prior leader. Lesson: when the wording says "Trump Commerce Secretary" and the relevant correspondence is contemporary Trump-world, the current/second-term Commerce Secretary can beat the older first-term holder even if the underlying Epstein date is old.
- Crans-Montana skier crash resolved to Lindsey Vonn, matching the lead. Lesson: high-profile comeback skiers deserve meaningful mass in injury/crash markets even when the event signal is thin.
- Champions League draw cluster resolved well after the Jan. 28 table/cross-market update: Real Madrid vs Benfica, Benfica vs Real Madrid, Newcastle first leg in Baku, and Galatasaray as the club that eliminates Juventus. Lesson: active future-dated market titles can contain useful cross-market clues; the Benfica-vs-Real racist-remark question was decisive evidence for the playoff draw.

Updates made:
- Pat Cummins replacement: Jan. 1 squad reporting showed Nathan Ellis was already in Australia's provisional squad, so he is unlikely to be the official replacement. Replaced the stale Ellis tail with outside pace options led by Sean Abbott, then Spencer Johnson, Ben Dwarshuis, Jason Behrendorff and Lance Morris.
- Australian Open men: Jan. 29 previews emphasized Sinner's five-match winning streak over Djokovic and Djokovic's lucky Musetti retirement/physical concerns, while Alcaraz remains setless against Zverev. Moved Alcaraz and Sinner level at 40%, lowered Djokovic.
- Super Bowl: both Maye and Darnold appeared on injury reports, offsetting some of the previous Maye-only move. Eased Seattle from 72% to 70%.

Watched but no update:
- Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, Grammy Album of the Year, market-fire city, strategic-minerals stockpile, Norway corruption, Libya killing/forensics, English League Cup final, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Jan. 29 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-30 Update

Resolution events:
- Pat Cummins replacement resolved to Ben Dwarshuis. The Jan. 29 correction helped by adding him, but I still overweighted Sean Abbott. Lesson: for Australia squad injury replacements in T20 conditions, left-arm domestic/franchise pace options such as Ben Dwarshuis and Spencer Johnson need more mass, especially when the obvious named seamers are already in the squad.

Updates made:
- Australian Open men: both semifinals went five sets. Alcaraz beat Zverev after a 5h27 match with cramp/medical-timeout concerns; Djokovic beat Sinner in five and reached the final. Moved the market to Alcaraz 60%, Djokovic 40%.
- Grammy Album of the Year: a Jan. 30 Independent preview tipped Bad Bunny's "Debi Tirar Mas Fotos" for Album of the Year, adding to earlier Lady Gaga/Kendrick arguments. Raised Bad Bunny to level with Kendrick and close behind Gaga.
- Super Bowl: Drake Maye missed Friday practice with a right-shoulder issue plus illness, though the Patriots said availability was not a major concern. Raised Seattle to 73%.

Watched but no update:
- Ukrainian miners-bus city, Balochistan secured town, market-fire city, strategic-minerals stockpile, Norway corruption, Libya killing/forensics, English League Cup final, Gaza mission troops, southern Lebanon abduction group and Myanmar panel had no Jan. 30 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-31 Update

Resolution events:
- Ukrainian miners-bus city resolved to Ternivka. I missed by focusing on larger Dnipropetrovsk mining/industrial cities such as Pavlohrad and Marganets. Lesson: for Dnipropetrovsk mining-transport attacks, include smaller coal-mining towns around Pavlohrad such as Ternivka and Pershotravensk.
- Balochistan secured town resolved to Nushki. I missed by sticking to Kalat/Khuzdar. Lesson: Balochistan multi-day battle/security-force questions need Nushki as a material town prior.
- Grammy Album of the Year resolved to Bad Bunny. The Jan. 30 Independent preview was the decisive late signal, but I left Gaga fractionally ahead. Lesson: when multiple previews split, a latest direct "will win AOTY" forecast close to the ceremony should move the named artist to at least co-lead.
- Australian Open men's title resolved to Carlos Alcaraz, matching the post-semifinal leader.

Updates made:
- None. Jan. 31 searches did not produce a strong named signal for active unresolved questions.

Watched but no update:
- Strategic-minerals stockpile name, Norwegian corruption investigation, market-fire city, Libya killing/forensics, English League Cup final, Clippers-Harden trade player, Brazilian bus-crash state, Myanmar panel, Ukrainian market shelling, Supreme Court map case, Super Bowl injuries, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Jan. 31 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-02-01 Update

Resolution events:
- Norwegian corruption investigation resolved to Thorbjorn Jagland. I missed by focusing on Erna Solberg's known stock-trading scandal. Lesson: former-PM corruption questions can point to older Labour/international figures, not just recent headline scandals.
- Strategic minerals stockpile resolved to Project Vault. I missed by forecasting descriptive government-reserve names. Lesson: U.S. strategic-stockpile project names can be branded/codenamed rather than bureaucratic.

Updates made:
- English League Cup final opponent: Feb. 1 coverage said Arsenal take a 3-2 lead into the Carabao Cup semi-final second leg against Chelsea, with the winner set up for a major final. Moved Man City's final opponent forecast to Arsenal 72%, Chelsea 28%.

Watched but no update:
- Libya killing/forensics, Clippers-Harden trade player, Brazil bus-crash state, Myanmar panel, market-fire city, Supreme Court map case, Ukrainian market shelling, Saleh Mohammadi execution city, Quentin Deranque MEP, Super Bowl, Gaza mission troops and southern Lebanon abduction group had no Feb. 1 signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-02-02 Update

Resolution events:
- English League Cup final opponent resolved to Arsenal, matching the Feb. 1 move.
- Myanmar's five-member panel resolved to Union Consultative Council. I missed by forecasting junta/security-style names. Lesson: Myanmar opposition/governance panels can use broad consultative/union branding, not only military-administration labels.
- Al Jazeera Arabic's Libya killing resolved to Saif al-Islam Gaddafi, and the related forensic-experts question resolved to Zintan. I had a low Saif tail and hit Zintan only partially. Lesson: linked Libya markets should be cross-updated aggressively when one leg resolves; investigation location is useful evidence for burial/funeral markets, but not identical to family/tribal burial place.
- Clippers-Harden trade player resolved to Darius Garland; Brazil bus-crash state resolved to Alagoas; huge market fire city resolved to Tehran. These were misses and reinforce that one-off incident questions need wider geographic/player tails.

Updates made:
- Saif burial town: added Zintan as a serious live outcome after the Zintan forensic-probe resolution, while keeping Bani Walid and Sirte ahead because burial likely follows family/tribal symbolism rather than only the killing site.
- Pakistan T20 World Cup markets: Feb. 2 reports say Pakistan will boycott the Feb. 15 India group match, would forfeit two points, and plays Netherlands, United States and Namibia in Group A. Replaced stale Ireland mass in the qualifying-win question with Namibia/United States/Netherlands, added Pakistan as the lead former champion at risk of missing the Super Eight, and raised Australia for Pakistan's likely Feb. 21 Super Eight opener.
- Manchester United/Carrick first defeat: Fulham did not hand Carrick his first defeat; United won 3-2 and Carrick is three-for-three. Removed Fulham and shifted mass to Tottenham, Liverpool, West Ham, Chelsea and "No team".
- AFC Women's Asian Cup: South Korea boycott-condition reporting plus the question background pointing to an Australia-South Korea semifinal moved Australia clearly ahead as the team likeliest to beat South Korea.
- Lebanon command-centre group: Feb. 2 Israeli reporting again named Hezbollah targets in southern Lebanon, so raised Hezbollah for the later command-centre question.

Watched but no update:
- Ukrainian market shelling, Supreme Court map case, Quentin Deranque MEP, Saleh Mohammadi execution city, Narges Mohammadi exile city, Washington Post publisher, Super Bowl, women's downhill silver, Gaza mission troops, Colombia peace talks, Lebanon abduction group and Bangladesh main opposition had no Feb. 2 signal strong enough to change the standing forecasts.

## 2026-02-03 Update

Resolution events:
- Ukrainian market shelling resolved to Druzhkivka. I missed by anchoring on Kostiantynivka/Kharkiv. Lesson: Donetsk market-shelling questions need smaller frontline cities such as Druzhkivka in the tail.
- Saif al-Islam Gaddafi burial resolved to Bani Walid. The Feb. 2 cross-update was helpful, and the burial did follow family/tribal symbolism over the Zintan investigation site.
- Supreme Court map case resolved to California. I missed by focusing on the usual Southern redistricting cases. Lesson: Republican-lawsuit map questions can point to blue-state congressional maps when the suit is a GOP challenge.
- Saleh Mohammadi public-execution city resolved to Qom. I missed by focusing on Kurdish-western cities. Lesson: Iranian sentencing/execution-location questions need Qom as a serious judicial/religious-city prior.
- Quentin Deranque MEP resolved to Rima Hassan. I missed by focusing on right-wing French figures. Lesson: violent-event questions involving far-right activists can center a left-wing or pro-Palestinian MEP as the speaker/target-adjacent figure.

Updates made:
- Women's downhill silver: Feb. 3 reports confirmed Lindsey Vonn fully ruptured her ACL but still plans to race; because she had been the season's leading downhiller, added United States while keeping Italy and Switzerland ahead.
- Grok/Dutch AI tool: UK/Ofcom/ICO reporting specifically named Grok in sexual-image enforcement concerns, so raised Grok strongly for the Dutch-court AI-tool market.
- Pakistan T20 markets: Feb. 3 reporting adde

/usr/bin/bash -lc "python - <<'PY'
import json,re
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('articles/2026/02/08/articles.jsonl')
patterns = {
 'school_shooting': r'(?i)(secondary school|school shooting|fatal mass shooting|deadly school shooting|La Loche|Brampton|Saskatchewan|Canada)',
 'sotu_tensions': r'(?i)(State of the Union|address|Trump.*address|late-February|tensions.*(Iran|Venezuela|China|Russia)|Iran.*tensions|Venezuela.*tensions|China.*tensions|Russia.*tensions)',
 'egypt_planning': r'(?i)(Egypt.*(planning|ministry|cabinet|reshuffle)|Rania Al-Mashat|Hala El-Said|planning minister)',
 'gezani': r'(?i)(Gezani|Madagascar.*cyclone|Cyclone Freddy|Cyclone Gafilo|landfall.*Madagascar)',
 'tractor': r'(?i)(Tractor FC|AFC Asian Champions League Elite|Al Nassr|Al Hilal|playoff)',
 'carrier': r'(?i)(USS Abraham Lincoln|carrier strike group|USS Gerald R\\. Ford|USS Harry S\\. Truman|aircraft carrier.*Middle East)',
 'deranque': r'(?i)(Deranque|Quentin|Lyon|Rima Hassan|Macron.*Meloni|French far-right activist|Charles Kushner|Pete Hoekstra)',
 'bangladesh': r'(?i)(Bangladesh.*(election|parliament|opposition|cabinet|minister)|Jamaat|BNP|National Citizen|Sharmeen|Rumana)',
 'rail': r'(?i)(burned cables|high-speed rail|Bologna|Venice|Milan|rail sabotage|Winter Olympics.*rail)',
 'navalny': r'(?i)(Navalny|Novichok|toxin|tissue samples)',
 'pakistan_bomb': r'(?i)(motorcycle bomb|police station|Bannu|North Waziristan|northwest Pakistan)',
 'sudan_market': r'(?i)(Sudan.*(market|drone strike)|El Obeid|Bara|crowded market)',
 't20': r'(?i)(T20 World Cup|Super Eight|Super Eights|Pakistan.*(Namibia|United States|India|Netherlands)|Namibia|India.*USA|boycott)',
 'storm': r'(?i)(Storm Marta|Storm Leonardo|Storm Leo|Storm Kristin|Storm Chandra|flooding in France|Western Europe.*storm)',
 'ronda': r'(?i)(Ronda Rousey|Holly Holm|Kayla Harrison)',
 'israel_bobsleigh': r'(?i)(Israel.*bobsleigh|bobsleigh.*Israel|commentary segment|BBC|CBC)',
 'benfica': r'(?i)(Benfica.*Real Madrid|Vinicius|racist remark|Otamendi|Florentino)',
 'russia_explosion': r'(?i)(military police facility|explosion.*St Petersburg|Gatchina|Vsevolozhsk)',
 'iran_frigate': r'(?i)(Iranian frigate|Sri Lanka|Dena|Sahand)',
 'dutch_ai': r'(?i)(Dutch court|sexual imagery|Grok|Midjourney|Stable Diffusion|DALL-E)',
 'dem_response': r'(?i)(Democratic response|State of the Union|Gretchen Whitmer|Andy Beshear|Hakeem Jeffries)',
 'sa_t20': r'(?i)(South Africa.*T20.*New Zealand|Aiden Markram|Heinrich Klaasen)',
 'lebanon_command': r'(?i)(command centre|Israel.*Lebanon|Hezbollah|Amal|Hamas)',
 'greek_migrant': r'(?i)(migrant boat.*capsized|Greek island|Chios|Lesbos|Samos|Kos|Crete)',
 'afghan_raids': r'(?i)(Pakistan.*air strikes.*Afghan|Paktika|Khost|condemns.*Pakistan)',
 'olympics_medals_hockey': r'(?i)(men.*ice hockey|ice hockey.*gold|Winter Olympics medal|30 total medals|Italy|Canada|United States|Sweden)',
 'brazil_flood': r'(?i)(southeastern Brazil|torrential-rain flooding|Rio de Janeiro|Sao Paulo)',
 'thai_ammo': r'(?i)(Thai ammunition|ammunition site|explosions.*Thailand|Royal Thai Army)',
 'uk_mosque': r'(?i)(mosque.*axe|tarawih|Birmingham|Manchester)',
}
compiled={k:re.compile(v) for k,v in patterns.items()}
results={k:[] for k in patterns}
for line in p.open():
    a=json.loads(line)
    title=str(a.get('title') or '')
    content=' '.join(str(a.get('content') or '').split())
    text=title+'\\n'+content
    for k,pat in compiled.items():
        if pat.search(text):
            results[k].append(a)
for k,arts in results.items():
    print('\\n###',k, len(arts))
    for a in arts[:10]:
        title=str(a.get('title') or '')
        content=' '.join(str(a.get('content') or '').split())
        text=title+' | '+content
        m=compiled[k].search(text)
        idx=max(0, (m.start() if m else 0)-180)
        snip=text[idx:idx+560]
        print('-', a.get('date_publish'), a.get('source'), title)
        print(' ', snip)
PY"

HEADLINE: Egypt reshuffles cabinet amid economic woes
SOURCE: www.rt.com
PUBLISHED: 2024-07-04 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2024-07-04 00:00:00
URL: https://www.rt.com/africa/600405-egypt-reshuffles-cabinet/

Egypt reshuffles cabinet amid economic woes

Egyptian President Abdel Fattah el-Sisi has overhauled his cabinet in an effort to tackle the mounting economic challenges facing the country’s 113 million people. On Wednesday, Sisi swore in former World Bank economist Ahmed Kouchouk as finance minister, succeeding Mohamed Maait, who had held the position since 2018. Kouchouk previously served as deputy finance minister, responsible for fiscal policy. Mostafa Madbouly, a member of the previously dissolved cabinet, took the oath of office at a ceremony in the capital, Cairo, following his reappointment as prime minister. Badr Abdelatty, Egypt’s ambassador to the European Union, has replaced Sameh Shoukry at the Foreign Ministry. Sisi named Karim Badawi as petroleum minister and Mahmoud Esmat as electricity minister, a role crucial for tackling the Arab nation’s persistent power outages. General Abdel Majeed Saqr is the defense minister. The former minister of international cooperation, Rania al-Mashat, was reappointed as minister of planning, economic development, and international cooperation. Sherif Farouk, chairman of Egypt Post, has taken over the Supply Ministry, which oversees grain purchases in Egypt – one of the world’s largest wheat importers. Farouk will also be in charge of a food subsidy program that feeds more than 60 million people, which the government has pledged to reform. Sisi won a third term in the country’s December election and was sworn into office in April. He has pledged continued development amid a severe economic crisis resulting from currency devaluation and rising prices, exacerbated by Israel’s war against Hamas in neighboring Gaza. The desert nation has sought increased investment, including from Russia, in order to address the hardships. In January, Egypt joined the BRICS economic group, along with the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Ethiopia, and Iran.

HEADLINE: Egypt set to change finance, foreign and supply ministers
SOURCE: www.khaleejtimes.com
PUBLISHED: 2024-07-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2024-07-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.khaleejtimes.com/hi/world/mena/egypt-set-to-change-finance-foreign-and-supply-ministers

Egypt set to change finance, foreign and supply ministers

Egypt is expected to change its foreign, finance, petroleum, electricity and supply ministers in a long-awaited cabinet overhaul, with Ahmed Kouchouk promoted to lead the finance ministry, state TV said on Tuesday. Kouchouk was serving as a deputy to Mohamed Maait, who as finance minister since 2018 played a central role in Egypt's negotiations with the International Monetary Fund as well as efforts to manage an economic crisis that included soaring inflation and foreign exchange shortages. At the foreign ministry, state TV citing local channel ExtraNews, reported that Egypt's ambassador to the European Union Badr Abdelatty would replace Sameh Shoukry. Shoukry is seen as a diplomatic heavyweight who had held the foreign portfolio since 2014, handling a flurry of diplomatic activity linked to the conflict in Gaza, on Egypt's northeastern border. Sherif Farouk, chairman of state postal company Egypt Post, was set to take over at the supply ministry, replacing veteran minister Ali Moselhy, the reports said. The supply ministry oversees Egypt's trade in wheat and other commodities, as well as a sprawling subsidy system for which tens of millions of Egyptians are eligible. It will be headed by Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly, who has been in the post since 2018. Ministers in Egypt have limited decision-making authority, with real power residing with the presidency, military and security services.

Egypt overhauls cabinet as economic pressures, power cuts persist

Egyptian President Abdel Fattah Al Sisi swore in a reshuffled cabinet on Wednesday that includes new finance and foreign ministers in a government facing challenges including the Gaza war on its border, economic woes and persistent power cuts. When the resignation of the previous cabinet was announced a month ago, Sisi reappointed Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly and said his new government should focus on lowering inflation and regulating markets as well as boosting investment. Sworn in as the new finance minister was Ahmed Kouchouk, formerly deputy finance minister and facing perhaps the biggest challenge in managing a stumbling economy and a debt burden that has soared in recent years. Egypt's sovereign dollar bonds rallied on Wednesday, with the longer-dated maturities gaining the most. The 2047 maturity rose by 1.96 cents to trade at 75.16 cents on the dollar by 1124 GMT. At the foreign ministry, Egypt's ambassador to the European Union, Badr Abdelatty, replaces Sameh Shoukry, a veteran who has steered Egypt's diplomatic efforts to contain the impact of the war in Gaza. Karim Badawi was appointed petroleum minister, Mahmoud Esmat electricity minister and General Abdel Majeed Saqr defence minister. SUBSIDY REFORM
Earlier this year, the country signed a record investment agreement with the United Arab Emirates and international funding deals including an expanded loan programme with the IMF. On Tuesday, another investment deal was announced for a high-end development on the Mediterranean coast.

HEADLINE: Egypt swears in a new Cabinet as mounting economic challenges fuel public discontent
SOURCE: www.independent.co.uk
PUBLISHED: 2024-07-03 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2024-07-04 00:00:00
URL: https://www.independent.co.uk/news/cabinet-ap-egypt-cairo-middle-east-b2573265.html

Egypt swears in a new Cabinet as mounting economic challenges fuel public discontent

Egypt’s new Cabinet was sworn in Wednesday as the country faces an ailing economy and raging conflicts in neighboring nations. The new Cabinet, chaired by Prime Minister Mustafa Madbouly, who has been in office since 2018, included major changes, particularly in the defense and economy-related portfolios. Some ministers, including those in charge of police, health and transportation, remain in place. The change comes as the country faces mounting public discontent following years of economic mismanagement, the coronavirus pandemic and the fallout of the wars in Europe and the Middle East. In addition, a Western-backed reform program — adopted in 2016 and backed by President Abdel Fattah el-Sissi — has caused prices to soar due to austerity measures. Nearly 30% of Egyptians currently live in poverty, according to official figures. Mohamed Maait, the country’s finance minister since 2018, was replaced by his deputy Ahmed Kouchouk, a former World Bank economist who played a major role in implementing a reform program with the International Monetary Fund. Earlier this year, the government floated the pound and sharply increased the main interest rate. Commercial banks are now trading the U.S. currency at more than 47 pounds, up from about 31 pounds. The measures are meant to combat ballooning inflation and attract foreign investment. This also came as attacks by Yemen’s Houthis on shipping routes in the Red Sea have slashed Suez Canal revenues
Egypt has been struggling to revive the lucrative tourism sector decimated by years of turmoil, the pandemic and most recently the war in Europe and the Middle East. In the new Cabinet, Sherif Fathy, a former civil aviation minister, replaced Ahmed Issa as Tourism and Antiquities Minister in the new government. Another main point of contention is the recurring power cuts during the soaring heat of summer in the country of over 106 million people. Consequently, Mahmoud Esmat was named to oversee the electricity ministry.

HEADLINE: Denmark has a record 25 government ministers after a major reshuffle
SOURCE: www.independent.co.uk
PUBLISHED: 2024-08-29 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2024-08-29 00:00:00
URL: https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/mette-frederiksen-ap-denmark-copenhagen-one-b2603827.html

Denmark has a record 25 government ministers after a major reshuffle

Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen Thursday appointed six new ministers in a major reshuffle of her three-party centrist government, bringing the number of ministers to 25 — the largest ever in 30 years. One of the ministers will head a new government office of emergency preparedness, climate change and cyber crimes. “The world has become more troubled,” Frederiksen said as she announced that Torsten Schack Pedersen will be in charge of the new ministry, after the reshuffled cabinet was formally presented to King Frederik X. Denmark now joins Sweden to have such a department. Besides the new emergency office, the government now has two other new ministries, bringing the total to 25, the largest since the 1993-1994 Cabinet of Social Democratic Prime Minister Poul Nyrup Rasmussen who had 24 ministers. Marie Bjerre left her job as minister for digitalization and gender equality, to become in charge of the European Union affairs, ahead of Denmark’s rotating EU presidency in the second half of 2025. Jeppe Bruus, the outgoing taxation minister, was brought in to head a brand new ministry, which will be coordinating Denmark’s green policy. The Scandinavian country has a goal of 70% emissions reductions by 2030 and achieving net zero by 2050. Bruus was replaced by Rasmus Stoklund while Bjerre's previous job was given to Caroline Stage Olsen, who became the digitalization minister. The gender equality portfolio was handed over to Environment Minister Magnus Heunicke. In one of the major surprises, Copenhagen mayor Sophie Hæstorp Andersen was appointed as minister in charge of social affairs instead of Pernille Rosenkrantz-Theil, who left the government. Frederiksen, who is the leader of the Social Democratic Party, announced Wednesday that she was doing a government reshuffle and creating three new ministries. In 2022, she presented the current majority government, consisting of her center-left Social Democrats, the center-right Liberal Party and the newly created centrist Moderates party.

Ivory Coast appoints largely unchanged new government

ABIDJAN - Ivory Coast President Alassane Ouattara appointed a new government of 31 ministers on Tuesday with many positions unchanged after unexpectedly removing the prime minister and his cabinet from office earlier this month. Robert Beugre Mambe, a close ally of the president and an influential figure in the ruling party, had been named prime minister on Monday after Ouattara removed his predecessor Patrick Achi and his cabinet on Oct. 6 without explanation. As part of the new appointments announced on Tuesday, Minister of Economic Planning and Development Niale Kaba is now heading a new combined ministry of Economy, Economic Planning and Development. Former Economy Minister Adama Coulibaly was appointed as Finance and Budget Minister, but ministers for Defence, Agriculture and Mines retained their roles. REUTERS

Sherif Farouk, chairman of Egypt Post, has taken over the Supply Ministry, which oversees grain purchases in Egypt – one of the world’s largest wheat importers. Farouk will also be in charge of a food subsidy program that feeds more than 60 million people, which the government has pledged to reform. Sisi won a third term in the country’s December election and was sworn into office in April. He has pledged continued development amid a severe economic crisis resulting from currency devaluation and rising prices, exacerbated by Israel’s war against Hamas in neighboring Gaza. The desert nation has sought increased investment, including from Russia, in order to address the hardships. In January, Egypt joined the BRICS economic group, along with the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Ethiopia, and Iran.

HEADLINE: Egypt set to change finance, foreign and supply ministers
SOURCE: www.khaleejtimes.com
PUBLISHED: 2024-07-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2024-07-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.khaleejtimes.com/hi/world/mena/egypt-set-to-change-finance-foreign-and-supply-ministers

Egypt set to change finance, foreign and supply ministers

Egypt is expected to change its foreign, finance, petroleum, electricity and supply ministers in a long-awaited cabinet overhaul, with Ahmed Kouchouk promoted to lead the finance ministry, state TV said on Tuesday. Kouchouk was serving as a deputy to Mohamed Maait, who as finance minister since 2018 played a central role in Egypt's negotiations with the International Monetary Fund as well as efforts to manage an economic crisis that included soaring inflation and foreign exchange shortages. At the foreign ministry, state TV citing local channel ExtraNews, reported that Egypt's ambassador to the European Union Badr Abdelatty would replace Sameh Shoukry. Shoukry is seen as a diplomatic heavyweight who had held the foreign portfolio since 2014, handling a flurry of diplomatic activity linked to the conflict in Gaza, on Egypt's northeastern border. Sherif Farouk, chairman of state postal company Egypt Post, was set to take over at the supply ministry, replacing veteran minister Ali Moselhy, the reports said. The supply ministry oversees Egypt's trade in wheat and other commodities, as well as a sprawling subsidy system for which tens of millions of Egyptians are eligible. It will be headed by Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly, who has been in the post since 2018. Ministers in Egypt have limited decision-making authority, with real power residing with the presidency, military and security services.

Egypt overhauls cabinet as economic pressures, power cuts persist

Egyptian President Abdel Fattah Al Sisi swore in a reshuffled cabinet on Wednesday that includes new finance and foreign ministers in a government facing challenges including the Gaza war on its border, economic woes and persistent power cuts. When the resignation of the previous cabinet was announced a month ago, Sisi reappointed Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly and said his new government should focus on lowering inflation and regulating markets as well as boosting investment. Sworn in as the new finance minister was Ahmed Kouchouk, formerly deputy finance minister and facing perhaps the biggest challenge in managing a stumbling economy and a debt burden that has soared in recent years. Egypt's sovereign dollar bonds rallied on Wednesday, with the longer-dated maturities gaining the most. The 2047 maturity rose by 1.96 cents to trade at 75.16 cents on the dollar by 1124 GMT. At the foreign ministry, Egypt's ambassador to the European Union, Badr Abdelatty, replaces Sameh Shoukry, a veteran who has steered Egypt's diplomatic efforts to contain the impact of the war in Gaza. HEADLINE: Al-Nassr to face Persepolis in Asian Champions League opener
SOURCE: www.straitstimes.com
PUBLISHED: 2023-08-24 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2023-08-24 00:00:00
URL: https://www.straitstimes.com/sport/football/al-nassr-to-face-persepolis-in-asian-champions-league-opener

Al-Nassr to face Persepolis in Asian Champions League opener

Cristiano Ronaldo's Saudi Pro League side Al-Nassr will kick off their Asian Champions League group stage challenge in Tehran against Iran's Persepolis next month while title holders Urawa Red Diamonds will take on Chinese champions Wuhan Three Towns. Japan's Urawa, who had to defeat Hong Kong's Lee Man in a playoff to secure their place in Thursday's draw in Kuala Lumpur, will also face 2021 runners-up Pohang Steelers from South Korea and Vietnam's Hanoi FC in Group J. Al-Nassr also had to qualify through the playoffs and, after a late 4-2 win over Shabab Al-Ahli of the United Arab Emirates, the Luis Castro coached outfit will also play Qatar's Al-Duhail and FC Istiklol from Tajikistan in Group E. Four-time winners Al-Hilal, who signed Neymar from Paris Saint-Germain last week, will start their challenge in Group D against Navbahor of Uzbekistan before taking on Iran's FC Nassaji Mazandaran and Mumbai City from India. The group phase of the continental competition will kick off on Sept. 18 and sees clubs divided into five groups each in the western and eastern regions. Winners of the 10 groups will advance to the knockout rounds alongside the three best runners-up in the western and eastern halves of the draw. The knockout rounds will commence on Feb. 12 with the final, played between the best club from their respective sides of the continent, to be held over two home-and-away legs on May 11 and 18.

URL: https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/sports/asian-champions-al-hilal-face-foolad-al-shabab-meet-al-duhail-2023-02-21/

Asian champions Al Hilal to face Foolad as Al Shabab meet Al Duhail

Feb 21 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia's Al Hilal will continue the defence of their Asian Champions League title against Foolad from Iran on Thursday while compatriots Al Shabab take on Al Duhail from Qatar. Riyadh-based Al Hilal, aiming to win the title for a record fifth time, were drawn to take on the team from Khuzestan in the quarterfinals after handing Shabab Al Ahli of the United Arab Emirates a 3-1 defeat in the round of 16 on Monday. Foolad will be appearing in the last eight of the competition for the first time, having defeated another Saudi Arabian side, Al Faisaly, on Sunday. Both quarterfinals will be played in Doha. The draw sets up the prospect of an all-Saudi semi-final on Sunday, with the winner going on to face Japan's Urawa Red Diamonds in the final over two legs on April 29 and May 6. Al Shabab advanced to the quarterfinals by defeating Nasaf Qarshi of Uzbekistan while Al Duhail eliminated compatriots Al Rayyan. Urawa booked their place in the tournament's decider in August and the first leg of the final will be hosted by the winners of Sunday's semi-final, with the return played at Saitama Stadium in Japan seven days later.

HEADLINE: Dates set for DP World ILT20 season 2 across the UAE
SOURCE: www.arabnews.com
PUBLISHED: 2023-08-24 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2023-08-24 00:00:00
URL: https://www.arabnews.com/node/2360616/sport

Dates set for DP World ILT20 season 2 across the UAE

Some of the clubs may not be too familiar to the likes of Neymar, Cristiano Ronaldo and Karim Benzema but these superstars now know who they will be facing in the 2023-24 AFC Asian Champions League. The draw in Kuala Lumpur on Thursday was a low-key affair but all of the 40 teams who start on this journey — with the tournament now an autumn-to-spring affair — know that the quartet of Saudi Arabian clubs are the ones to beat. With Al-Nassr squeezing through the playoffs with a 4-2 win over Shabab Al-Ahli on Tuesday and going into Pot 4, any group they entered was always going to earn that well-known cliched tag. There is no “death” here though as Group E is alive with talent, history and excitement. It contains Iranian titans Persepolis, one of the biggest clubs in Asia, and there could be 100,000 fans in Tehran to greet the Yellows when they arrive. That will be quite the clash and will show those who are new to Asian football what the competition is all about. Al-Duhail are not only the champions of Qatar but reached the semifinals last time around and have Hernan Crespo as coach. The other team in the group is far from a whipping boy too. Istiklol of Tajikistan beat Al-Hilal in 2021 and cannot be underestimated. Al-Nassr will be fancied to go through given the talent at coach Luis Castro’s disposal but the Portuguese boss, who has had a poor start to the domestic season with two defeats from two, may end up wishing that he had been handed an easier ride. Al-Hilal’s certainly looks to be a smoother ride to the last 16 and their three opponents have just one previous appearance in the tournament between them. It is less than four months since the Blues lost the final to Urawa Reds and missed the chance to extend their record of four wins to five. Their Iranian opponents are FC Nassaji Mazandaran. They are little-known outside the country and are making their continental debut after winning the Hafzi Cup.

HEADLINE: Neymar, Benzema to get first taste of Asian Champions League action
SOURCE: www.france24.com

Neymar, Benzema to get first taste of Asian Champions League action

Al-Nassr's Cristiano Ronaldo and Al-Ittihad's Karim Benzema will also debut in the group stage of Asia's premier club football competition, where they will seek to power their clubs to victory against sides from central to east Asia. With billions of dollars in oil wealth, Saudi Arabia has been on a buying spree of sports assets as part of plans to diversify its economy and attract tourism and investment. Al-Hilal leads the AFC Champions League for most title wins, with four to its name. With the flamboyant Brazilian playmaker Neymar in their ranks for the 2023-24 season, Al-Hilal hope to win the title for a record fifth time. Al-Nassr, semi-finalists in the 2020 and 2021 editions, will play Iran's Persepolis FC, Al-Duhail SC of Qatar and Tajikistan's FC Istiklol. Saudi Pro League champions Al-Ittihad will have their sights set on a first Asian Champions League title since winning back-to-back titles in 2004 and 2005 after securing the services of French stars Benzema and N'Golo Kante. They will take on Iran's Sepahan SC, Air Force Club of Iraq and Uzbekistan's AGMK FC. Defending champions Urawa Red Diamonds of Japan, bidding for the title from the competition's eastern division, will face China's Wuhan Three Towns FC, Pohang Steelers of South Korea and Vietnam's Hanoi FC. After the draw at the Asian Football Confederation headquarters in Kuala Lumpur, secretary-general Windsor John said all of the qualified teams faced tough ties across the competition. "No easy matches," he told AFP, adding that "a video assistant referee (VAR) system will be introduced from the group stage". The group stage will run from September 18 to December 13, with the knockout stage to kick off on February 12. The two-leg final will be played on May 11 and 18, 2024.

What happens if Pakistan pull out of 2026 T20 World Cup? ICC sanctions at stake, India’s February 15 opponent in focus

Hours after the decision on Saturday, PCB chairman Mohsin Naqvi said Bangladesh had been unfairly treated by the ICC, citing the India-Champions Trophy precedent from last year, before adding that Pakistan would now reconsider its own participation in the tournament. Having stood by BCB throughout the dispute, Naqvi said the Pakistan government would take the final and binding call on whether the national team travels for the World Cup. The reports further claimed Pakistan had assured Bangladesh it would review its own participation if the issue remained unresolved. Speculation followed that the team was asked to halt preparations for the tournament and that the PCB had begun working on contingency plans in the event of a boycott. While PCB officials stayed silent amid the speculation, their backing of Bangladesh was confirmed after the ICC ejected the Tigers from the 2026 T20 World Cup. “Bangladesh is a big stakeholder and they have been unfairly treated in this case. I maintained this in Wednesday’s meeting as well,” Naqvi told reporters. “One country is dictating. When the ICC changed venues for Pakistan and India as a favour, why was the same not done for Bangladesh?”
Naqvi added that Pakistan’s final decision would be taken once Prime Minister Shahbaz Sharif, currently out of the country, returns. “Whether we play in the T20 World Cup or not, the decision will be taken by the government,” he said. “When the prime minister returns, we will take his advice. The government’s decision will be final and binding. If they say no, the ICC may invite another team.”
Ranked 21st in T20Is, Uganda would be playing the T20 World Cup for the second time, having made their debut in 2024 after qualifying through the Africa regional qualifiers. Placed in Group C in that edition, they registered their maiden World Cup win against Papua New Guinea before finishing fourth after losses to West Indies, New Zealand and Afghanistan. Uganda’s inclusion would also mean they take the February 15 slot against India in Ahmedabad.

HEADLINE: Suspected sabotage and arson hits Italy on Winter Olympics’ opening day
SOURCE: www.independent.co.uk
PUBLISHED: 2026-02-07 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-02-07 00:00:00
URL: https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/europe/winter-olympics-bologna-train-arson-b2915980.html

Suspected sabotage and arson hits Italy on Winter Olympics’ opening day

Rail infrastructure near Bologna was targeted by saboteurs on Saturday morning, causing significant disruption to train services on the opening day of the Winter Olympic Games. Police in Italy reported three distinct incidents across different locations, leading to delays of up to two-and-a-half hours for high-speed, Intercity, and regional services. The state-owned railway, Ferrovie dello Stato (FS), was forced to temporarily close its high-speed station in Bologna as a result. The Transport Ministry, led by Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini, condemned the acts as "serious sabotage."
It drew parallels to similar incidents that affected France's high-speed TGV network on the opening day of the 2024 Summer Olympic Games in Paris. The ministry asserted: "These actions of unprecedented seriousness do not in any way tarnish Italy's image in the world, an image that the Games will make even more compelling and positive."
By Saturday afternoon, rail traffic was reported to be slowly returning to normal across the affected network. Police said a cabin housing a track switch was set on fire before dawn near the Adriatic city of Pesaro. Several hours later, electrical cables used to detect train speeds were found severed in Bologna, while a rudimentary explosive device was discovered by a track at a nearby location. A spokeswoman said no one had claimed responsibility for the incidents, which appeared to have been coordinated. Bologna is a major junction for Italy's east-to-west rail lines, and is also the key hub linking the south to northern cities such as Milan and Venice. Milan is a co-host of the Winter Games alongside Cortina, which is reachable via train from Venice. Elsewhere, thousands of people took to the streets of Milan on Saturday in a protest over housing costs and environmental concerns on the first full day of the Olympic Games.

Winter Olympics in Italy hit by train disruptions as police suspect sabotage

ROME - Italian police said on Feb 7 they suspected arsonists had struck rail infrastructure near the northern city of Bologna, triggering major disruptions to train traffic on the first full day of the Winter Olympic Games. The fire hit a track switch and severed electrical cables used to detect train speed, causing delays of up to two hours for high-speed, Intercity and regional services at one of Italy’s busiest rail hubs. The blaze was “presumably deliberate” but had not been claimed, a spokesperson for the police said, adding that transport police and the anti-terrorist Digos force were on site and investigating. The fire targeted the line between Bologna and Venice, but also caused knock-on problems for traffic between Bologna and Milan, and routes to the Adriatic coast. Milan is a co-host for the Winter Games alongside Cortina, which is reachable via train from Venice. At the 2024 Summer Olympic Games staged in Paris, saboteurs struck France’s TGV high-speed train network in a series of pre-dawn attacks across the country, causing travel chaos ahead of the opening ceremony. Italy’s state-owned railway Ferrovie dello Stato (FS) said that despite the disruption, trains were still running. REUTERS

PUBLISHED: 2024-07-26 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2024-07-26 00:00:00
URL: https://www.independent.co.uk/news/french-ap-paris-gabriel-attal-emmanuel-macron-b2586500.html

What we know about 'malicious' attack on French train network ahead of Olympics opening

French transport was thrust into chaos Friday just hours ahead of the Olympics 2024 opening ceremony after a series of coordinated "malicious acts” upended high-speed train lines. Officials said the arson attacks and other vandalism on the rail system were a form of sabotage on a pivotal day of the Games. There was no sign of a direct connection to the Olympics, but authorities are investigating. Travel for hundreds of thousands of travelers was disrupted on the rail system linking Paris to the rest of France and to neighboring countries. No injuries have been reported. Here is what to know:
What happened to the rail network? Details were still emerging, but officials say that there were arson attacks and other “criminal acts” of vandalism. Transport Minister Patrice Vergriete said people fled fires and incendiary devices were discovered. Authorities said they had found at least three fires, triggering disruptions especially affecting Paris' Montparnasse station. “Everything indicates that these are criminal fires,” Vergriete said. French Prime Minister Gabriel Attal said intelligence services are trying to find the perpetrators of “acts of sabotage” that he called “prepared and coordinated.”
They happened against a backdrop of global tensions and heightened security measures in Paris as the city prepared for the 2024 Olympic Games. Paris prosecutors launched investigations into crimes that could carry 15- to 20-year prison sentences. Jean-Pierre Farandou, CEO of rail company SNCF, said on French TV network BFMTV that the acts showed “a desire to seriously harm” the French and that their nature implied “a premeditated, calculated, coordinated attack.”
How will arson attacks affect the 2024 Olympics? French trains and the larger transport system faced delays, with some trips taking hours longer than they normally do or halted altogether. The delays rippled out internationally to stations in London.
